event: Stone tools and remains from sites such as Dara-i-Kur indicate human habitation in the region during the Middle Paleolithic.
Mundigak and other settlements flourish as early Bronze Age urban centers linked to the Indus Valley and Mesopotamian trade networks.
The Bactria-Margiana Archaeological Complex (Oxus Civilization) develops sophisticated cities and metallurgy in northern Afghanistan.
Indo-Iranian peoples settle the region, and the area later associated with Bactria emerges as an early center of Zoroastrian tradition.
Cyrus the Great incorporates Bactria, Aria, and Arachosia into the Achaemenid Persian Empire as satrapies.
Alexander the Great conquers the region, founding cities including Alexandria in Arachosia (Kandahar) and campaigning through Bactria.
After Alexander's death the territory falls under the Seleucid Empire amid struggles with the Mauryan Empire to the east.
Diodotus I declares independence from the Seleucids, founding the Greco-Bactrian Kingdom centered in northern Afghanistan.
Greco-Bactrian and later Indo-Greek rulers spread Hellenistic culture, exemplified by the city of Ai-Khanoum.
Nomadic Yuezhi and Scythian incursions overrun Bactria, ending sustained Greek rule in the north.
The Kushan Empire arises under the Yuezhi, making the region a hub of Buddhism and Silk Road commerce.
Kanishka the Great expands the Kushan Empire, patronizing Gandharan Buddhist art and culture.
The Sasanian Persians subdue much of the region, establishing Kushano-Sasanian rule.
The Hephthalites (White Huns) invade and dominate the region, disrupting Sasanian and Indian control.
A Sasanian-Gokturk alliance destroys Hephthalite power, dividing the territory between rival empires.
Arab Muslim armies advance into the region following the defeat of the Sasanians at the Battle of Nahavand.
The Saffarid dynasty under Ya'qub ibn al-Layth rises from Sistan, asserting Persianate Muslim rule across the region.
Sabuktigin establishes the Ghaznavid dynasty at Ghazni, founding a powerful Turkic Islamic empire.
Mahmud of Ghazni launches the first of many campaigns into northern India, making Ghazni a center of wealth and learning.
The Ghurids sack Ghazni, and the Ghurid dynasty rises to dominate the region and expand into northern India.
Genghis Khan's Mongol armies devastate Balkh, Herat, and Bamiyan during the Mongol conquest.
Timur (Tamerlane) brings the region into the Timurid Empire, which makes Herat a flourishing center of art and scholarship.
Babur captures Kabul and makes it his base before founding the Mughal Empire in India in 1526.
Mirwais Hotak leads a successful revolt at Kandahar, establishing the Hotaki dynasty and Afghan independence from Safavid Persia.
Nader Shah of Persia captures Kandahar and Kabul, subduing the Afghan tribes during his westward and eastward campaigns.
Ahmad Shah Durrani is proclaimed king at Kandahar, founding the Durrani Empire and the modern Afghan state.
Timur Shah Durrani moves the Afghan capital from Kandahar to Kabul.
Dost Mohammad Khan consolidates power in Kabul, founding the Barakzai dynasty.
The First Anglo-Afghan War begins as British forces invade to install a friendly ruler amid the Great Game with Russia.
The catastrophic British retreat from Kabul ends the First Anglo-Afghan War in disaster for Britain.
The Second Anglo-Afghan War erupts over British concerns about Russian influence in Kabul.
Abdur Rahman Khan becomes Amir, and the Treaty of Gandamak leaves Britain in control of Afghan foreign affairs.
The Durand Line is drawn, demarcating the contentious border between Afghanistan and British India.
The Third Anglo-Afghan War leads to the Treaty of Rawalpindi, granting Afghanistan full independence in foreign affairs under Amanullah Khan.
King Amanullah introduces Afghanistan's first constitution and a program of modernizing reforms.
A rebellion forces Amanullah to abdicate, and Nadir Khan seizes the throne after a period of upheaval.
Mohammed Zahir Shah becomes king after the assassination of his father, beginning a four-decade reign.
Afghanistan joins the United Nations as a founding-era member state.
Zahir Shah promulgates a liberal constitution, ushering in a brief period of constitutional monarchy and democratic experimentation.
Mohammed Daoud Khan overthrows the monarchy in a coup and declares Afghanistan a republic.
The communist Saur Revolution topples Daoud and brings the People's Democratic Party of Afghanistan to power.
The Soviet Union invades Afghanistan, igniting a decade-long war against the mujahideen insurgency.
Soviet forces complete their withdrawal, leaving the Najibullah government to fight on against the mujahideen.
The fall of Najibullah's government leads to mujahideen rule and a devastating civil war among rival factions.
The Taliban capture Kabul and establish the Islamic Emirate, imposing a strict interpretation of Islamic law.
A U.S.-led coalition topples the Taliban after the September 11 attacks, and the Taliban destroy the Bamiyan Buddhas earlier that year.
A new constitution is adopted and Hamid Karzai is elected president of the Islamic Republic of Afghanistan.
Ashraf Ghani becomes president as NATO formally ends its combat mission, transferring security to Afghan forces.
The United States and the Taliban sign the Doha Agreement setting terms for a U.S. troop withdrawal.
The Taliban recapture Kabul amid the U.S. withdrawal, reestablishing the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan.
